---
title: "Concepts"
description: "Source vs output, harnesses, progressive disclosure"
group: "Authoring"

---
## Source vs output

skillprism separates **source** (what you author) from **output** (what agents read).

**Source** lives in your project:

```
skills/dice-roller/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ skill.yaml       # Metadata: name, description, variables, config
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ SKILL.md         # Template: MiniJinja, rendered once per harness
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ references/      # Optional asset directories (copied verbatim)
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ scripts/
```

**Output** is generated by `skillprism build`, one copy per harness:

```
.claude/skills/dice-roller/SKILL.md      # Claude Code
.opencode/skills/dice-roller/SKILL.md    # OpenCode
.agents/skills/dice-roller/SKILL.md      # Codex
```

You never edit output files directly ‚Äî they're always a build artifact. Change the source, re-run `skillprism build`, and every harness's copy is regenerated.

## skill.yaml and SKILL.md: two halves of one skill

`skill.yaml` holds **metadata** ‚Äî name, description, license, variables, and harness-specific config. `SKILL.md` is a **[MiniJinja](https://docs.rs/minijinja) template** that gets rendered with that metadata. They're two halves of one skill:

- `skill.yaml`'s `name` and `description` become `{{ skill_name }}` and `{{ skill_description }}` in the template.
- `skill.yaml`'s `variables:` entries are available by name in the template.
- Every other `skill.yaml` field (`license`, `when_to_use`, `allowed_tools`, etc.) is available under its own name.

The template can also be named `SKILL.md.j2` if you'd rather the extension say "this is a template" explicitly. Both are accepted, but not both at once in the same skill directory ‚Äî skillprism errors rather than guessing which one you meant.

## Harnesses

A **harness** is an agent product that reads skills: Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, OpenCode, Factory, Pi. Each has its own:

- **Output path** ‚Äî where it expects to find skill files (e.g. `.claude/skills/` vs `.opencode/skills/`)
- **Capabilities** ‚Äî what features it supports (subagents, allowed-tools, manifests, frontmatter modes)
- **Length caps** ‚Äî max name/description lengths (e.g. Claude allows 1536-char descriptions; the spec's portable cap is 1024)
- **Macros** ‚Äî harness-specific template variables exposed as `{{ harness.<name> }}`

You configure which harnesses your project targets in `skillprism.yaml`. `skillprism build` renders every skill once per configured harness.

## Capability gating

Not every harness supports every feature. A skill can declare `required-capabilities` in `skill.yaml`:

```yaml
required-capabilities:
  - subagent
  - allowed-tools
```

If a harness doesn't support a required capability, that skill-harness pair is **skipped** (not a build failure) ‚Äî every other pair still builds. You'll see a `[resolve] skipped: ...` warning on stderr.

## Progressive disclosure

The [Agent Skills spec](https://agentskills.io/specification) uses **progressive disclosure** ‚Äî agents load skill metadata at startup, full instructions only when a task matches, and reference files on demand:

1. **Metadata** (~100 tokens): `name` and `description` are loaded at startup for all skills
2. **Instructions** (<5000 tokens recommended): The full `SKILL.md` body loads when the skill activates
3. **Resources** (as needed): Files in `references/`, `scripts/`, `assets/` load only when required

Keep your main `SKILL.md` under 500 lines. Move detailed reference material to separate files in subdirectories ‚Äî skillprism copies every subdirectory of a skill verbatim alongside the rendered `SKILL.md`.
